children.name: Image
children.description: This element describes the actual Image that was acquired and its meta-data. The elements that are references (ending in Ref or Settings) refer to elements defined outside of the Image element. Ref elements are simple links, while Settings elements are links with additional values. If any of the required Image attributes or elements are missing, its guaranteed to be an invalid document.

children.name: ID
children.description: A unique identifier for this Image.

children.name: Name
children.description: A user assigned name for this Image.

children.name: Acquisition Date
children.description: The Date in which this Image was Acquired.

children.name: Instrument Ref
children.description: This empty element refers to the Instrument that was utilized to acquire this Image.

children.name: ID
children.description: A unique identifier for this Instrument.

children.name: Experiment Ref
children.description: This empty element refers to the Experiment during which this Image was acquired.

children.name: ID
children.description: A unique identifier for this Experiment.

children.name: Experimenter Ref
children.description: This empty element refers to Experimenter that performed this experiment.

children.name: ID
children.description: A unique identifier for this Experimenter.

children.name: Sample Ref
children.description: This empty element refers to the Sample that was imaged in this Image Acquisition.

children.name: ID
children.description: A Unique Identifier for this element.

children.name: Experimenter Group Ref
children.description: This empty element refers to the ExperimenterGroup that owns this Image.

children.name: ID
children.description: A unique identifier for this ExperimenterGroup.

children.name: ID
children.description: A unique identifier for this ROI.

children.name: Stage Insert Ref
children.description: This empty element refers to the optional Stage Insert device that was used for the Acquisition of this Image.

children.name: Stage Insert ID
children.description: A Unique Identifier for this component.

children.name: Focus Stabilization Device Ref
children.description: This empty element refers to the optional Focus Stabilization Device that was used for the Acquisition of the experimental Image this Mechanical Calibration procedure refers to.

children.name: ID
children.description: A Unique Identifier for this component.

children.name: Acquisition Software Ref
children.description: This empty element refers to the Software that was used to drive the Microscope during the Image Acquisition.

children.name: ID
children.description: A Unique Identifier for this element.

children.name: Description
children.description: This element holds a free text multi-line optional description for this component.

children.name: Imaging Environment
children.description: This element describes the environment in which the biological sample was maintained during the Acquisition of this Image.

children.name: ID
children.description: This is the Unique Identifier of the Environmental Control Device used in this case.

children.name: Temperature
children.description: The field records the Temperature at which this Image Acquisition was performed. Allowed values include a number or the phrase "Room Temperature".

children.name: CO2Percent
children.description: The field records the Carbon Dioxide concentration (expressed as a % value) at which this Image Acquisition was performed. Allowed values include a number or the phrase "Ambient Conditions".

children.name: Humidity
children.description: The field records the Humidity (expressed as a % value) at which this Image Acquisition was performed. Allowed values include a number or the phrase "Ambient Humidity".

children.name: Air Pressure
children.description: The field records the AirPressure the Acquisition of this Image was performed at.

children.name: O2Percent
children.description: This field records the Oxygen concentration around the sample [units:none] during the Acquisition of this Image. Expressed as a percentage fraction as a value from 0.0 to 1.0.

children.name: Description
children.description: This element holds a free text multi-line optional description for this component.

children.name: Map
children.description: This is a Mapping of Key/Value pairs.

children.name: M
children.description: This is a Key/Value pair used to build up a Mapping. The Element and Attribute Name are kept to single letters to minimize the length at the expense of readability as they are likely to occur many times.

children.name: K
children.description: This is the key of this Key/Value pair.

children.name: Microscope Stand Settings
children.description: This element refers to the Microscope Stand used to acquire this Image and it holds the Settings that were applied to the Microscope Stand during this Acquisition.

children.name: ID
children.description: This is the Unique Identifier of the Microscope used in this case.

children.name: Total Effective Magnification
children.description: This fields records the Total Effective Magnification used in this case. This number can be obtained by multiplying Magnification Changer:Magnification times the Objective:NominalMagnification or even better the Objective:CalibratedMagnification if available.

children.name: Field Of View
children.description: The Field of View defines the area of the specimen that is viewable via the eyepiece. This field caputers the measures size of the Field of View.

children.name: Map Annotation

children.description: This field recods whether the Microscope Table was Floating (True) or not (False) during the acquistion of this Image.

children.name: Objective Settings
children.description: This element holds the Setting applied to the Objective used during the Acquisition of this Image as well as a reference (the ID attribute) to the Objective in question.

children.name: ID
children.description: This is the Unique Identifier of the Objective used in this case.

children.name: Correction Collar Position
children.description: The Correction Collar typically consists of an adjustable ring on the Objective whose position can be modified. This field records the position of the collar during the Acquisition of this Image. Each correction collar has an arbitrary scale or it might be scale-less and instead have fixed positions with qualitative designations (i.e., Oil, Water in case in which the Objective might be corrected for Immersion Liquid).

children.name: Numerical Aperture Setting
children.description: In case the Numerical Aperture (NA) of this Objective is variable (i.e., as for example in case of Objectives that can be used with or without Cover Glass or Objectives that can be dipped in different media), this field capture the NA setting that was employed for Image Acquisition. NOTE: please note that often NA regulation in these types of Objectives occurs via a rotating collar whose exact position is difficult to measure and reproduce across different Experiments.

children.name: Measured Refractive Index
children.description: The field records the Temperature dependent measured Refractive Index of the Immersion Liquid (or Dipping Medium) utilized in this case.

children.name: Temperature
children.description: This field captures the Temperature of the Objective if different from the ImagingEnvironment/Temperature.

children.name: Iris Aperture
children.description: This field records the aperture of the Iris diaphragm, which determines the size of the objective rear aperture yielding a variable numerical aperture range between 0.5 and the objective's upper limit.

children.name: Immersion Liquid
children.description: This element refers to the Immersion Liquid that was used with this Objective during the Acquisition of this Image. According to ISO 10934-1:2002, an Immersion Liquid is a liquid (commonly immersion oil, water, glycerol or any media in which the Specimen is submerged during acquisition in case a Cover Glass is not used) specified as suitable for use in the space between the front of an Objective lens and the Cover Glass or the Object itself (i.e., such as is the case for Dipping Objectives). Refer to ISO 8036-1 for more details on Immersion Oil. Please NOTE: Because the Immersion Liquid is considered in the computing of corrections to be part of the lens, its refractive index and dispersion (or Abbe number) are critical. Also please NOTE: In case a Cover Glass is not used during Acquisition the Immersion Liquid is most often referred to as Dipping Medium. Examples of Dipping Media include, water-based solutions (biological buffers, culture media, other water-based solutions) and organic-based [FocusClear (CLARITY); CUBIC-1, CUBIC-2, CUBIC-R1, CUBIC-R2; 2,2-thiodiethanol (TDE); ethyl cinnamate; Benzyl Benzoate and BB-PEG (PEGASOS); Benzyl Alcohol, Benzyl Benzoate (BABB); dibenzyl ether (DBE); Ethanol].

children.description: This field describes the XY Velocity that was set for this Sample Positioning Device during the Acquisition of this experimental Image.

children.name: ZVelocity Setting
children.description: This field describes the Z-axis Velocity that was set for this Sample Positioning Device during the Acquisition of this experimental Image.

children.name: XYReproducibility Settings
children.description: This field describes the XY Precision that was set for this Sample Positioning Device during the Acquisition of this experimental Image. This metric refers to the capacity this Device of maintaining a given XY Position over time as specified by the Manufacturer. This metric is generally referred to as Reproducibility and it might also be referred to as Lateral Drift. This metric is particularly relevant for Stereology systems and for the calibration of Sample Positioning devices.

children.name: ZReproducibility Settings

This metric refers to the capacity this Device of maintaining a given Z-axis Position over time as specified by the Manufacturer. This metric is generally referred to as Reproducibility and it might also be referred to as Focal Drift. This metric is particularly relevant for Stereology systems and for the calibration of Sample Positioning devices.

children.name: Optical Calibration
children.description: This describes the procedure that was used for Optical Calibration and the resulting Calibration measurements.

children.name: ID
children.description: A Unique Identifier for this component.

children.name: Date
children.description: Ths field records the Date in which this Calibration procedure was performed.

children.name: Theoretical XYSpot Size_FWHM
children.description: This field captures the Theoretical (expected) lateral spot size expressed as the Full Width at Half Maximum (FWHM) of the Point Spread Function (PSF) intensity profile fitted using a Gaussian function, on the basis of the given optical conditions. This is a measure of the expected resolution of the system in the xy-plane. This value should be calculated in a manner as to be compared to the Median XY Spot Size_FWHM.

children.name: Median XYSpot Size_FWHM
children.description: An important way of evaluating the effective resolution of any Microscope, consists in measuring the size and shape of the observed Point Spread Function (PSF) generated by imaging one or more fluorescent Point-like objects (i.e., Point Emitters) under conditions that are identical to those used to Acquire a given experimental Image. More specifically and according to ISO 21073:2019, lateral Resolution is defined by the FWHM of the intensity signal along a lateral direction through the centre of a fluorescent Point-like object (i.e., Spot or Point Emitter) positioned at the center of the Field of View (FOV). This field captures the average Observed Median lateral (i.e., in the xy-plane) Spot Sze expressed as the Full Width at Half Maximum (FWHM) of the Point Spread Function (PSF) intensity profile fitted using an Elliptic Gaussian function. Ideally this measurement should be reflective of multiple individual Point Emitter observations across the Field of View (FOV). However, if a single measurement is taken, it is essential that this be taken from the center of the Objective FOV.

children.name: Min XYSpot Size_FWHM
children.description: An important way of evaluating the effective resolution of any Microscope, consists in measuring the size and shape of the observed Point Spread Function (PSF) generated by imaging one or more fluorescent Point-like objects (i.e., Point Emitters) under conditions that are identical to those used to Acquire a given experimental Image. More specifically and according to ISO 21073:2019, lateral Resolution is defined by the FWHM of the intensity signal along a lateral direction through the centre of a fluorescent Point-like object (i.e., Spot or Point Emitter) positioned at the center of the Field of View (FOV). This field captures the average Observed Minimum lateral (i.e., in the xy-plane) Spot Sze expressed as the Full Width at Half Maximum (FWHM) of the Point Spread Function (PSF) intensity profile fitted using an Elliptic Gaussian function. Ideally this measurement should be reflective of multiple individual Point Emitter observations across the Field of View (FOV). However, if a single measurement is taken, it is essential that this be taken from the center of the Objective FOV.

children.name: Max XYSpot Size_FWHM
children.description: An important way of evaluating the effective resolution of any Microscope, consists in measuring the size and shape of the observed Point Spread Function (PSF) generated by imaging one or more fluorescent Point-like objects (i.e., Point Emitters) under conditions that are identical to those used to Acquire a given experimental Image. More specifically and according to ISO 21073:2019, lateral Resolution is defined by the FWHM of the intensity signal along a lateral direction through the centre of a fluorescent Point-like object (i.e., Spot or Point Emitter) positioned at the center of the Field of View (FOV). This field captures the average Observed Maximum lateral (i.e., in the xy-plane) Spot Sze expressed as the Full Width at Half Maximum (FWHM) of the Point Spread Function (PSF) intensity profile fitted using an Elliptic Gaussian function. Ideally this measurement should be reflective of multiple individual Point Emitter observations across the Field of View (FOV). However, if a single measurement is taken, it is essential that this be taken from the center of the Objective FOV.

children.name: Theoretica ZSpot Size_FWHM
children.description: This field captures the Theoretical (expected) z-axial spot size expressed as the Full Width at Half Maximum (FWHM) of the Point Spread Function (PSF) intensity profile fitted using a Gaussian function, on the basis of the given optical conditions. This is a measure of the expected resolution of the system in the z-plane. This value should be used calculated in a manner as to be compared to the median Z spot size.

children.name: Median ZSpot Size_FWHM
children.description: An important way of evaluating the effective resolution of any Microscope, consists in measuring the size and shape of the observed Point Spread Function (PSF) generated by imaging one or more fluorescent Point-like objects (i.e., Point Emitters) under conditions that are identical to those used to Acquire a given experimental Image. More specifically and according to ISO 21073:2019, axial Resolution is defined by the FWHM of the intensity signal along the axial direction through the centre of a fluorescent Point-like object (i.e., Spot or Point Emitter) positioned at the center of the Field of View (FOV). This field captures the average Observed Median Z-axial Spot Sze expressed as the Full Width at Half Maximum (FWHM) of the Point Spread Function (PSF) intensity profile fitted using an Elliptic Gaussian function. Ideally this measurement should be reflective of multiple individual Point Emitter observations across the Field of View (FOV). However, if a single measurement is taken, it is essential that this be taken from the center of the Objective FOV.

children.name: Checked NA
children.description: In case the Numerical Aperture (NA) of the Objective utilized to perform this Optical Calibration is variable (i.e., as for example in case of Objectives that can be used with or without Cover Glass or Objectives that can be dipped in different media), this field capture whether (True) or not (False) a manual check was performed to confirm that the Numerical Aperture was set to the Maximum allowable value. This check is necessary because often NA regulation in these types of Objectives occurs via a rotating collar whose exact position is difficult to measure and reproduce across different Experiments. In this context, setting the collar to the Maximum NA position is desirable not only because it is more easy to reproduce and it best to estimate the maximal resolution of the system.

children.name: Z-Dimension
children.description: This field records whether (True) or not (False) this Optical Calibration procedure was performed using XY or XYZ Images of the Optical Standard sample.

children.name: Nyquist Rate
children.description: This field captures the ratio between the Pixel Size and the Nyquist Criterion. The Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem establishes that 'when sampling a signal (e.g., converting from an analog signal to digital), the sampling frequency must be greater than twice the Band Width of the input signal in order to be able to reconstruct the original perfectly from the sampled version' (Whittaker E.T., (1915). Proc. Roy. Soc. Edinburgh Sect. A, 25:181; Shannon C.E., (1949) Proc. IRE, 37:10). The Nyquist Criterion determines the minimal Sampling Density needed to capture ALL information from the Specimen into the Image. It can be shown that if the Sampling Density is higher than the Nyquist Rate (also referred to as Critical Sampling Distance) all information about the object is captured. It is important to note that the Nyquist Rate depends on the Objective Numerical Aperture (NA) and on the Excitation and Emission WaveLengths. The recommended Nyquist Rate is > 2 for conventional light microscopy, and it is 2.5 - 3 for high resolution imaging.

children.name: Sample Size
children.description: This field captures the Sample size that was used to calculate the reported Optical Calibration metrics. For example, this might represent the number of Colored Beads that were utilized to gather data on the Optical performance of the Microscope.

children.name: PSFEquation
children.description: This field captures the Equation that was used to perform the Eliptical PSF Gaussian Fit.

children.name: Planarity
children.description: This is a measure of the flatness of the focal plane across the Field of View (FOV). The planarity of the focal plane is calculated from the observed z-position of the best focus of each Point Emitter. It indicates focal aberrations or tilt. Ideally this measurement should be reflective of multiple individual Point Emitter observations across the Field of View (FOV). However, if a single measurement is taken, it is essential that this be taken from the center of the Objective FOV.

children.name: Lateral Asymmetry
children.description: This field reports the mean +/- standard deviation over the Field of View (FOV) of the observed Point Spread Function (PSF) Lateral Asymmetry (i.e. the ratio of the minimum lateral FWMH over the maximum lateral FWHM). It indicates the presence of optical aberrations. Ideally this measurement should be reflective of multiple individual Point Emitter observations across the Field of View (FOV). However, if a single measurement is taken, it is essential that this be taken from the center of the Objective FOV.

children.name: Axial Asymmetry
children.description: This field reports the mean +/- standard deviation over the Field of View (FOV) of the observed Point Spread Function (PSF) Axial Asymmetry (i.e. the ratio of the minimum axial FWMH over the maximum axial FWHM; also referred to as Spherical Aberration). It indicates the presence of optical aberrations. Ideally this measurement should be reflective of multiple individual Point Emitter observations across the Field of View (FOV). However, if a single measurement is taken, it is essential that this be taken from the center of the Objective FOV.

children.name: Chromatic Registration Evaluation
children.description: According to ISO 21073:2019, co-registration accuracy measures the capacity of a microscope system to image a fluorescent object with different excitation and emission Wavelengths at the same position in the Image. Hence, the spatial distance of the Image positions of a Point-like object (i.e., Spot or Point Emitter) located in the centre of the Field of View (FOV) for different excitation and detection Wavelength bands is denoted as co-registration accuracy. The co-registration accuracy shall be calculated from the Intensity maxima or the Intensity centre of gravity of the Image of the Point-like object for the different Wavelengths. The lateral co-registration accuracy is expressed as: ∆lateral = √(xλ1 - xλ2)^2 + (yλ1 - yλ2)^2; and the axial co-registration accuracy is expressed as: ∆axial = √(zλ1 - zλ2)^2. This element refers to the results of a specific Co-registration Accuracy evaluation assay associated with this Optical Calibration procedure.

children.name: ID
children.description: A Unique Identifier for this component.

children.name: Reference Wavelength
children.description: This field captures the Wavelength of the Reference Channel against which the Chromatic Registration Accuracy is being calculated in this procedure.

children.name: Test Wavelength
children.description: This field captures the Wavelength of the Test Channel whose Chromatic Registration Accuracy is being calculated in this procedure.

children.name: XChromatic Shift_RT
children.description: This field captures the offset in the the observed X-coordinate of the Intensity maxima or the Intensity centre of gravity of the Image of a Point-like object (i.e., Spot or Point Emitter) when comparing the Reference vs. the Test Wavelengths. This shall be calculates as: √(xλT - xλR)^2, where This offset could be reported either in number of Pixels or in physical Distance, when a sub-Pixel offset needs to be calculated.

children.name: YChromatic Shift_RT
children.description: This field captures the offset in the the observed Y-coordinate of the Intensity maxima or the Intensity centre of gravity of the Image of a Point-like object (i.e., Spot or Point Emitter) when comparing the Reference vs. the Test Wavelengths. This shall be calculates as: √(xλT - xλR)^2, where This offset could be reported either in number of Pixels or in physical Distance, when a sub-Pixel offset needs to be calculated.

children.name: XChromatic Shift_TR
children.description: This field captures the offset in the the observed X-coordinate of the Intensity maxima or the Intensity centre of gravity of the Image of a Point-like object (i.e., Spot or Point Emitter) when comparing the Test vs. the Reference Wavelengths. This shall be calculates as: √(xλR - xλT)^2, where This offset could be reported either in number of Pixels or in physical Distance, when a sub-Pixel offset needs to be calculated.

children.name: YChromatic Shift_TR
children.description: This field captures the offset in the the observed Y-coordinate of the Intensity maxima or the Intensity centre of gravity of the Image of a Point-like object (i.e., Spot or Point Emitter) when comparing the Test vs. the Reference Wavelengths. This shall be calculates as: √(xλR - xλT)^2, where This offset could be reported either in number of Pixels or in physical Distance, when a sub-Pixel offset needs to be calculated.

children.name: Axial Chromatic Shift_RT
children.description: This field captures the offset in the the observed Z-coordinate of the Intensity maxima or the Intensity centre of gravity of the Image of a Point-like object (i.e., Spot or Point Emitter) when comparing the Reference vs. the Test Wavelengths. This shall be calculates as: √(zλT - zλR)^2, where This offset could be reported either in number of Pixels or in physical Distance, when a sub-Pixel offset needs to be calculated.

children.name: Axial Chromatic Shift_TR
children.description: This field captures the offset in the the observed Z-coordinate of the Intensity maxima or the Intensity centre of gravity of the Image of a Point-like object (i.e., Spot or Point Emitter) when comparing the Test vs. the Reference Wavelengths. This shall be calculates as: √(zλT - zλR)^2, where This offset could be reported either in number of Pixels or in physical Distance, when a sub-Pixel offset needs to be calculated.

children.name: Field Uniformity Evaluation
children.description: This describes the procedure that was used to measure the Field dependent variations in the pixel-to-pixel sensitivity of the detector and/or by distortions in the optical path.

children.name: ID
children.description: A Unique Identifier for this component.

children.name: Max Intensity Pixel XCoordinate
children.description: This field reports the x-axis coordinate of the Pixel that has the Maximum Intensity in the Image used for this Flat Field Calibration procedure.

children.name: Max Intensity Pixel YCoordinate
children.description: This field reports the y-axis coordinate of the Pixel that has the Maximum Intensity in the Image used for this Flat Field Calibration procedure.

children.name: Mean Maxto Corner Intensity Ratio
children.description: This field reports the Mean between the Ratios obtained by comparing the Maximum Pixel Intensity and the Intensities of the four corner Pixels of the Image used for this Flat Field Calibration procedure.

children.name: SDMaxto Corner Intensity Ratio
children.description: This field reports the Standard Deviation between the Ratios obtained by comparing the Maximum Pixel Intensity and the Intensities of the four corner Pixels of the Image used for this Flat Field Calibration procedure.

children.name: Flat Field Image Ref
children.description: This is an empty element that refers to an Image obtained from a Standard Slide designed to eavluate the Field of View (FOV) dependent variations in the pixel-to-pixel sensitivity of the detector and/or by distortions in the Optical Path.

children.name: ID
children.description: A Unique Identifier for this component.

children.name: Optical Calibration Standard Group
children.description: This element refers to the Optical Calibration Standard used in this case. This might be a pre-manufactured slide, such as those produced by Chroma (https://www.chroma.com/products/accessories/92001-autofluorescent-plastic-slides), Argolight (http://argolight.com/), Geller MicroAnalytical Laboratory (Magnification Reference Standard MRS-4 and others; http://www.gellermicro.com/mag_standards/mrs.html), Zeiss and others. Alternatively, the standard might be a slide containing multi-colored beads of known size and emission characteristics. Finally, slides coated with specific DNA origami patterns are increasingly being used for this purpose as well.

children.name: Fluorescent Reference Slide
children.description: This element describes a Fluorescent Reference Slide. These Slides provide a uniform fluorescent field and therefore they enable to check how evenly a Excitation Light Source is filling the Field of View (FOV), and whether it is centered. These Slides are easy to use an cheap. However they are typically made of plastic and it is often difficult to ensure the reproducibility of their emission properties. These Slides are mostly known to be produced by Chroma, but similar products are available from Ted Pella or Thorlabs.

children.description: This is true if the binary data was written in BigEndian order. This is dependent on the system architecture of the machine that wrote the pixels. True for essentially all modern CPUs other than Intel and Alpha. All Binary data must be written in the same endian order.

children.name: Calibration Standard Slide
children.description: This element describes a Standard Calibration Slide (also referred to as Standard Test Slide) used for assessing the Optical properties of the Microscope that was used to acquire this experimental Image. These Slides can be used to measure multiple different Optical properties including resolution, chromatic registration, magnification, distance, and different kinds of optical distorsions.

children.description: The type of calibration slide used in this case. Examples include slides produced by Argolight, Geller MicroAnalytical Laboratory, PSFCheck, Zeiss and others.

children.name: Calibration Measure
children.description: This field captures the Optical characteristic this Slide is intended to calibrate, examples include Co-registration Accuracy, Detector linearity, Distance, Magnification, Optical distorsion, Resolution, or Multi.

children.name: Imaged Area
children.description: This field describes the specific region or sets or regions of the Calibration Slide that have been used for this Optical Calibration procedure.

children.name: Calibration Software
children.description: This element describes a software tool utilized to analyze Calibration metrics obtained from this Calibration Procedure and generate a suitable Calibration Reports. As an example, such a Software tool might analyzes microscopy images of fluorescent Colored Beads in order to quantify the Resolution and Chromatic Registration of a fluorescence microsocope over the full Field of View (FOV). An example of such a tool is PSFj (http://www.knoplab.de/psfj/sample-page/). Alternatively, such a Software tool might be used to extract Intensity Calibration results obtained with a specific Intensity Calibration Tool, such as a Power Meter or MetaMax (https://www.umassmed.edu/grunwaldlab/).

children.name: Annotation Ref
children.description: This is a simple multi-line comment or annotation describing this component.

children.name: Detector Calibration
children.description: This element describes a procedure used to measures the performance of the Dectector used to to Acquire this experimental Image.

children.name: ID
children.description: A Unique Identifier for this component.

children.name: Date
children.description: Ths field records the Date in which this Calibration procedure was performed.

children.name: Sample Size
children.description: This field captures the Size of the Sample that was used to calculate the reported Intensity Calibration metrics. For example, this might represent the number of Illumination Power measurements that were utilized to calculate the Illumination Stability of a given Light Source.

children.name: Method
children.description: This field records a Description of Method used for this calibration procedure.

children.name: Intensity Calibration Tool Ref
children.description: This element is a reference to the tool, if any, used to perform this calibratin procdure

children.name: ID
children.description: A Unique Identifier for this component.

children.name: Intensity Calibration Report
children.description: This element refers to any File containing a report generated by the Calibration Software referred to in Calibration procedure.

children.name: Calibration Map
children.description: This element describes a 2D matrix containing values computed as a resulut of the calibration procedure. This matrix is the same size of the experimental Image this calibration refers to.

children.name: Metric Type
children.description: This field captures the type of metric that was computed as part of the calibratin procedure in this instance.

children.name: Illumination Intensity
children.description: This attribute only applies to matrices of type Variance and Mean. It represents the illumination intensity that was used to acquire the images used to contruct this matrix. The value can range from 0 (i.e. dark image) to any intenisty value experssed either as percentage or as absolute power.

children.name: Nr Of Images
children.description: This field represents the number of images that were employed to generate this calibration matrix.

children.name: Row Nr
children.description: The index of this Row.

children.name: Cell Nr
children.description: The index of this Cell.

children.name: Cell Value
children.description: The Value of the metric contained in this Cell.

children.name: Description
children.description: This element holds a free text multi-line optional description for this component.

children.name: Detector Calibration Image Ref
children.description: This is an empty element that refers to an Image (typically a z-stack) obtained as part of this Detector Calibration Procedure used in this instance and Acquired using the same Instrument and Settings employed to acquire the experimental Image this calibration refers to.

children.name: ID
children.description: A Unique Identifier for this component.

children.name: Pixels
children.description: The Pixels element is going to be removed in the future, but it is still required. This is just notice that the contents of Pixels will be moved up to Image in a future release. This is because there has only been 1 Pixels object in each Image for some time. The concept of multiple Pixels sets for one Image failed to take off. It is therefore redundant. The Image will be unreadable if any of the required Pixel attributes are missing. The Pixels themselves can be stored within the OME-XML compressed by Plane, and encoded in Base64. Or the Pixels may be stored in TIFF format. The Pixels element should contain a list of BinData or TiffData, each containing a single Plane of pixels. These Pixels elements, when read in document order, must produce a 5-D Pixel array of the size specified in this element, and in the dimension order specified by 'DimensionOrder'. All of the values in the Pixels object when present should match the same value stored in any associated TIFF format (e.g. SizeX should be the same). Where there is a mismatch our readers will take the value from the TIFF structure as overriding the value in the OME-XML. This is simply a pragmatic decision as it increases the likelihood of reading data from a slightly incorrect file.

children.name: ID
children.description: A Unique Identifier for this component.

children.name: Dimension Order
children.description: This field records the order in which the individual Planes of data are interleaved in the Image.

children.name: Size X
children.description: This field records the X size of the Pixel data array.

children.name: Size Y
children.description: This field records the Y size of the Pixel data array.

children.name: Size Z
children.description: This field records the number of Z-Planes acquired for this Image.

children.name: Size C
children.description: This field records the number of Channels used in this Image

children.name: Size T
children.description: This field records the number of Timepoints (often referred to as Frames) used in this Image.

children.name: Physical Size X
children.description: This field records the real world Physical Width represented by a Pixel in this Image. This measure is often referred to as X Pixel Size.

children.name: Physical Size Y
children.description: This field records the real world Physical Height represented by a Pixel in this Image. This measure is often referred to as Y Pixel Size.

children.name: Physical Size Z
children.description: This field records the real world Physical distance between the individual Z-Planes recorded for this Image. This measure is sometimes referred to as Z Pixel or Voxel Size.

children.name: Time Increment
children.description: In cases in which a time series is acquired with a global timing specification setting, this field describes the time between each sequential plane (or frame) in the series (i.e., from the start of time point 1 to the start of time point 2). Examples include, a video stream or a multi-dimensional Acquisition routine. Note that, this field is not intended to substitute the Timestamp attribute recorded in Plane. Also note that in case this field is not relevant e.g. a time lapse Acquisition with irregular increments, the value -1 should be used to indicate that only the Timestamps (in the Plane element) are valid.

children.name: Pixel Type
children.description: This field records the data type used to represent the Pixel Intensity in the Image.

children.name: Interleaved
children.description: This field records the order in which the Channels are arranged within the data block: True if Channels are stored RGBRGBRGB...; False if Channels are stored RRR...GGG...BBB…

children.name: Significant Bits
children.description: This field records the number of bits within the type storing each Pixel value that are significant. e.g.,. you can store 12-bit data within a 16-bit type. This does not reduce the storage requirements but can be a useful indicator when processing or viewing the Image data.

children.name: Big Endian
children.description: This field is True if the pixels data was written in BigEndian order. If this value is present, it should match the value used in BinData or TiffData. If it is not, a reader should be used that honors the value used in the BinData or TiffData. This value is useful for MetadataOnly files and is to allow for future storage solutions.

children.name: Plane
children.description: Each Image has to have at least one Plane and one Channel. The Plane element holds the Microscope stage and Image timing data for a given Channel/z-section/timepoint. The total number of Plane elements for a given Image must equal to SizeZ x SizeC x SizeT.

children.name: ID
children.description: A unique identifier for this Plane.

children.name: The Z
children.description: This field records the Z-section to which this Plane corresponds. This is numbered from 0.

children.name: The T
children.description: This field records th Timepoint to which this Plane corresponds. This is numbered from 0.

children.name: The C
children.description: This field records th Channel to which this Plane corresponds. This is numbered from 0.

children.name: Timestamp
children.description: This field records the time at which this Plane was acquired as recorded from the beginning of Image Acquisition.

children.name: Exposure Time
children.description: This field records the duration of the Exposure utilized to acquire this Plane.

children.name: Pixel Dwell Time
children.description: This value typically refers to the settings applied to the Confocal Scanner during Acquisition, and it assumes that Pixel Dwell Time was set uniformly throughout the Plane. The Pixel Dwell Time represents the time this Confocal Scanner was set to dwell upon each pixel during the acquisition of this Plane (e.g. 1.58 µsec/pixel). The higher the Pixel Dwell Time the slower is the scan speed, the higher the sensitivity but also the potential photobleaching.

children.name: Position X
children.description: This field records the X position of the Plane within the Microscope's reference frame. This value can typically be obtained by recording the Sample Positioning Device and Stage Insert coordinates.

children.name: Position Y
children.description: This field records the Y position of the Plane within the Microscope's reference frame. This value can typically be obtained by recording the Sample Positioning Device and Stage Insert coordinates.

children.name: Position Z
children.description: This field records the Z position of the Plane within the Microscope's reference frame. This value can typically be obtained by recording the Sample Positioning Device and Stage Insert coordinates.

children.name: Plane XOffset
children.description: This field records the X offset (expressed in Pixels) that specifies the position of this Plane's center with respect to the center of the full frame.

children.name: Plane YOffset
children.description: This field records the Y offset (expressed in Pixels) that specifies the position of this Plane's center with respect to the center of the full frame.

children.name: Plane ZOffset
children.description: This field describes the Z-offset imposed by the Focal Positioning device.

children.name: Hash SHA1
children.description: This optional element is a hash of the Plane's Image data. It is a choice between all the supported hash types. Currently the only method supported is SHA1

children.name: Mechanical Calibration
children.description: This describes the procedures that were used for mechanical calibration and the resulting calibration results.

children.name: ID
children.description: A Unique Identifier for this component.

children.name: Date
children.description: Ths field records the Date in which this Calibration procedure was performed.

children.name: Measured XReproducibility
children.description: This field captures the observed Precision with which this Instrument is capable of maintaining a given X-axis position over time during Image Acquisition and as measured by this Mechanical Calibration procedure. This metric is generally referred to as Reproducibility and it might also be referred to as X Lateral Drift. This metric is particularly relevant for Stereology systems and for the calibration of Sample Positioning devices.

children.name: Measured YReproducibility
children.description: This field captures the observed Precision with which this Instrument is capable of maintaining a given Y-axis position over time during Image Acquisition and as measured by this Mechanical Calibration procedure. This metric is generally referred to as Reproducibility and it might also be referred to as Y Lateral Drift. This metric is particularly relevant for Stereology systems and for the calibration of Sample Positioning devices.

children.name: Measured Focal Reproducibility
children.description: This field captures the observed Precision with which this Instrument is capable of maintaining a given Z-axis position over time during Image Acquisition and as measured by this Mechanical Calibration procedure. This metric is generally referred to as Reproducibility and it might also be referred to as Focal Drift. This metric is particularly relevant for Stereology systems and for the calibration of Sample Positioning devices.

children.name: Measured XRepeatability
children.description: This field captures the observed X-axis Accuracy with which this Instrument is capable of returning to a previously visited position during Image Acquisition and as measured by this Mechanical Calibration Procedure. This metric is important for example for Tiling.

children.name: Measured YRepeatability
children.description: This field captures the observed Y-axis Accuracy with which this Instrument is capable of returning to a previously visited position during Image Acquisition and as measured by this Mechanical Calibration Procedure. This metric is important for example for Tiling.

children.name: Measured Focal Repeatability
children.description: This field captures the observed Z-axis Accuracy with which this Instrument is capable of returning to a previously visited position during Image Acquisition and as measured by this Mechanical Calibration Procedure. This metric is important for example for Tiling.

children.name: Measured XSettling Time
children.description: This field captures the observed Time it takes for this Instrument to stabilize at the target X-axis position during Image Acquisition and as measured by this Mechanical Calibration Procedure.

children.name: Measured YSettling Time
children.description: This field captures the observed Time it takes for this Instrument to stabilize at the target Y-axis position during Image Acquisition and as measured by this Mechanical Calibration Procedure.

children.name: Measured Focal Settling Time
children.description: This field captures the observed Time it takes for this Instrument to stabilize at the target Z-axis position during Image Acquisition and as measured by this Mechanical Calibration Procedure.

children.name: Measured XReproducibility_SD
children.description: This field captures the Standard Deviation associated with the observed Precision with which this Instrument is capable of maintaining a given X-axis position over time during Image Acquisition and as measured by this Mechanical Calibration procedure.

children.name: Measured YReproducibility_SD
children.description: This field captures the Standard Deviation associated with the observed Precision with which this Instrument is capable of maintaining a given Y-axis position over time during Image Acquisition and as measured by this Mechanical Calibration procedure.

children.name: Measured Focal Reproducibility_SD
children.description: This field captures the Standard Deviation associated with the observed Precision with which this Instrument is capable of maintaining a given Z-axis position over time during Image Acquisition and as measured by this Mechanical Calibration procedure.

children.name: Measured XRepeatability_SD
children.description: This field captures the Standard Deviation associated with the observed X-axis Accuracy with which this Instrument is capable of returning to a previously visited position (useful for tiling) during Image Acquisition and as measured by this Mechanical Calibration Procedure.

children.name: Measured YRepeatability_SD
children.description: This field captures the Standard Deviation associated with the observed Y-axis Accuracy with which this Instrument is capable of returning to a previously visited position (useful for tiling) during Image Acquisition and as measured by this Mechanical Calibration Procedure.

children.name: Measured Focal Repeatability_SD
children.description: This field captures the Standard Deviation associated with the observed Z-axis Accuracy with which this Instrument is capable of returning to a previously visited position (useful for tiling) during Image Acquisition and as measured by this Mechanical Calibration Procedure.

children.name: Measured XSettling Time_SD
children.description: This field captures the Standard Deviation associated with the observed Time it takes for this Instrument to stabilize at the target X-axis position during Image Acquisition and as measured by this Mechanical Calibration Procedure.

children.name: Measured YSettling Time_SD
children.description: This field captures the Standard Deviation associated with the observed Time it takes for this Instrument to stabilize at the target Y-axis position during Image Acquisition and as measured by this Mechanical Calibration Procedure.

children.name: Measured Focal Settling Time_SD
children.description: This field captures the Standard Deviation associated with the observed Time it takes for this Instrument to stabilize at the target Z-axis position during Image Acquisition and as measured by this Mechanical Calibration Procedure.

children.name: Sample Positioning Ref
children.description: This empty element refers to the Sample Positioning device that was used for the Acquisition of the experimental Image this Mechanical Calibration procedure refers to.

children.name: ID
children.description: A Unique Identifier for this component.

children.name: Focus Stabilization Device Ref
children.description: This empty element refers to the optional Focus Stabilization Device that was used for the Acquisition of the experimental Image this Mechanical Calibration procedure refers to.

children.name: ID
children.description: A Unique Identifier for this component.

children.name: Annotation Ref
children.description: This is a simple multi-line comment or annotation describing this component.

children.name: The Additional Dimension Ref
children.description: This empty element refers to the Additional Dimension Element to which this Plane (or Chunk of Image data) corresponds.

children.name: ID
children.description: A Unique Identifier for this component.

children.name: Plane Transformation Matrix
children.description: This element describes a 4x4 matrix relating the position of this Plane typically with respect to other Planes within this Image or collection of Images (or alternatively to the Microscope's reference frame). This can correct for translations, rotations, scale differences, or other affine distortions between Planes from the same set or between Image Datasets. An example is when multiple cameras are used for e.g. to acquire different Channels for the same Image. See: https://www.openmicroscopy.org/community/viewtopic.php?p=17433.

children.name: ID
children.description: A Unique Identifier for this component.

children.name: Row Nr
children.description: The index of this Row.

children.name: Cell Nr
children.description: The index of this Cell.

children.name: Cell Value
children.description: The Value of the metric contained in this Cell.

children.name: Annotation Ref
children.description: This is an empty element that refers to an Annotation (typically a Comment Annotation that consists of a simple multi-line comment) describing this component.

children.name: ID
children.description: A Unique Identifier for this element.

children.name: Channel
children.description: Each Image has to have at least one Plane and one Channel. The Channel element stores information about how the each Image Channel was acquired. The total number of Channel elements for a given Image must equal to SizeC.

children.name: ID
children.description: A unique identifier for this Channel.

children.name: Name
children.description: A Name for this Channel that is suitable for presentation to the user.

children.name: Illumination Type
children.description: This field describes the Light Microscopy Illumination method performed in this case. Allowable terms were obtained from the Biological Imaging Methods Ontology (Fbbi; https://www.ebi.ac.uk/ols/ontologies/fbbi) Light Microscopy term (http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FBbi_00000345).

children.name: Illumination Type Term Acession
children.description: This field records the Accession ID for the term used to describe the Illumination Type. A sub-classes of Light Microscopy term (http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FBbi_00000345) is recommended.

children.name: Contrast Method
children.description: This field describes the technique used to achieve contrast for this Channel.

children.name: Color
children.description: This field records the Color used to render this Channel - encoded as RGBA. The default value is -1 corresponding #FFFFFFFF, so solid white (it is a signed 32 bit value).

children.name: Samples Per Pixel
children.description: This field is prevalently used for RGB Images and records the number of samples the detector takes to form each pixel value. [units:none]

children.name: Fluorophore
children.description: This element describes the Fluorophore whose Emission is used to produce this Channel. [plain text string].

children.name: Manufacturer
children.description: This field refers to the company, research group or individual who built or manufactured this hardware component.

children.name: Model
children.description: This field refers to the Model name or any other designation the Manufacturer uses for this component.

children.name: Catalog Number
children.description: This field records the Catalog, Part or Serial Number used by the Manufacturer to uniquely identify this component.

children.name: Lot Number
children.description: The Lot Number used by the Manufacturer to refer to a specific production batch or lot for this component.

children.name: ID
children.description: A Unique Identifier for this component.

children.name: Name
children.description: This field records a descriptive term that designates this Fluorophore as specified by the Manufacturer. For example, it might be the name of a specific Fluorescence Protein variant, or the name used by the Manufactuer for an organic dye.

children.name: Type
children.description: This field records a term that describes the general category of the Fluorescent Label used in this case.

children.name: Excitation Wavelength
children.description: This field records the Excitation Wavelength used for this Channel.

children.name: Emission Wavelength
children.description: This field records the Emission Wavelength used for this Channel.

children.name: Concentration
children.description: If applicable (e.g., in the case of direct incubation of the sample  with a specific label such as DAPI or Mitotracker Red), this field records the Concentration of this Fluorophore that was used to stain the Sample and that was imaged in this Channel.

children.name: FBBase ID
children.description: In case the Fluorophore is a Fluorescent Protein, this field records its FPBaseID (FPBase.org).

children.name: Primary Publication ID
children.description: This field captures the a Unique Identifier for the Primary Publication describing this Fluorescent Label. Examples include, Digital Object Identifier (DOI), PubMed Central Identifier (PMCID), ArXiv.org ID etc.

children.name: Map Annotation
children.description: A Map Annotation. The contents of this is a list of Key/Value pairs.

children.name: ID
children.description: A Unique Identifier for this element.

children.name: Namespace
children.description: We recommend the inclusion of a namespace for annotations you define. If it is absent then we assume the annotation is to use our (OME's) default interpretation for this type.

children.name: Annotator
children.description: The Annotator is the person who attached this annotation.e.g. If UserA annotates something with TagB, owned by UserB, UserA is still the Annotator.

children.name: Description
children.description: This element contains a description for the annotation.[plain-text multi-line string]

children.name: Annotation Ref
children.description: This is an empty element that refers to an Annotation (typically a Comment Annotation that consists of a simple multi-line comment) describing this component.

children.name: ID
children.description: A Unique Identifier for this element.

children.name: Value
children.description: This is a Mapping of Key/Value pairs.

children.name: M
children.description: This is a Key/Value pair used to build up a Mapping. The Element and Attribute Name are kept to single letters to minimize the length at the expense of readability as they are likely to occur many times.

children.name: K
children.description: This is the key of this Key/Value pair.

children.name: Specs File
children.description: This element refers to a PDF file that contains the specifications for this hardware component as provided by the Manufacturer.

children.name: ID
children.description: A Unique Identifier for this element.

children.name: Namespace
children.description: We recommend the inclusion of a namespace for annotations you define. If it is absent then we assume the annotation is to use our (OME's) default interpretation for this type.

children.name: Annotator

children.name: Description
children.description: This element contains a description for the annotation.[plain-text multi-line string]

children.name: Annotation Ref
children.description: This is an empty element that refers to an Annotation (typically a Comment Annotation that consists of a simple multi-line comment) describing this component.

children.name: ID
children.description: A Unique Identifier for this element.

children.name: Binary File
children.description: Describes a binary file.

children.name: Size
children.description: Size of the uncompressed file. [unit:bytes]

children.name: External
children.description: Describes a file location. Can optionally specify a portion of a file using Offset and a ReadLength. If Offset and ReadLength are specified in conjuction with Compression, then they point into the uncompressed file.

children.name: href
children.description: file location

children.name: SHA1
children.description: The digest of the file specified in href.

children.name: Compression
children.description: Specifies the compression scheme used to encode the data.

children.name: Bin Data
children.description: The contents of this element are base64-encoded. These are not CDATA sections, just a base64 stream.

children.name: Compression
children.description: Specifies the compression scheme used to encode the data.

children.name: Big Endian
children.description: This is true if the binary data was written in BigEndian order. This is dependent on the system architecture of the machine that wrote the pixels. True for essentially all modern CPUs other than Intel and Alpha. All Binary data must be written in the same endian order.

children.name: Length
children.description: Character count attribute for the BinData field. This is the length of the base-64 encoded block. It allows easy skipping of the block when parsing the file. [unit:bytes]

children.name: Additional Vendor Information
children.description: This element is intendended to be used as a last-resort solution to capture vendor-specific Additional Information about this hardware component, which cannot be described using either available model elements (Classes or Attributes) or the Map Annotation feature of the schema. The use of this feature should not be intended as a way to hide information that the Imaging Community has designated as essential to ensure rigor and reproducibility. In case this feature is used, the Vendor is fully responsible for this information, including but not limited to, making sure this information is appropriately recorded and kept up to date.

children.name: ID
children.description: A Unique Identifier for this model element.

children.name: Vendor Name
children.description: The Name of the Vendor responsible for this Information.

children.name: Vendor Bin Data
children.description: The contents of this element are base64-encoded. These are not CDATA sections, just base64 streams.

children.name: Compression

children.description: This is true if the binary data was written in BigEndian order. This is dependent on the system architecture of the machine that wrote the pixels. True for essentially all modern CPUs other than Intel and Alpha. All Binary data must be written in the same endian order.

children.name: Length
children.description: Character count attribute for the BinData field. This is the length of the base-64 encoded block. It allows easy skipping of the block when parsing the file. [unit:bytes]

children.name: Annotation Ref
children.description: This is an empty element that refers to an Annotation (typically a Comment Annotation that consists of a simple multi-line comment) describing this component.

children.name: ID
children.description: A Unique Identifier for this element.

children.name: Light Path
children.description: This element holds a description of the Light Path associated with the Acquisition of this Channel.

children.name: ID
children.description: A Unique Identifier for this component.

children.name: Name
children.description: A user-defined Name for this component.

children.name: Light Source Settings
children.description: This element holds the Setting applied to a Light Source used in the Light Path during the acquisition of this Channel as well as a reference to the LightSource (the ID attribute) in question.

children.name: ID
children.description: A Unique Identifier for this component.

children.name: Illumination Power Settings
children.description: This field records the nominal Illumination Power (also known as Power Output or Illumination Intensity) that was set for the Acquisition of this Image or was reported by the Image Acquisition Software, as advertised by the Manufacturer. Depending on the system this might be reported as a % value.

children.name: Attenuation
children.description: This field records the actual Attenuation of the excitation light power that was applied during the Acquisition of this Channel, expressed as a fractional value from 0.0 to 1.0.

children.name: Attenuation Method
children.description: This field records the Method that was used to Attenuate the Laser beam during the acquisition of this Image.

children.name: Used Acousto Optical Device
children.description: In case a Laser was used for this Image Acquisition, this field records whether (True) or not (False) an AcoustoOptical Device was employed for intensity modulation.

children.name: Light Source Coupling Ref
children.description: This empty element refers to a LightSourceCoupling component used in this Ligth Path.

children.name: Excitation Filter Ref
children.description: This field refers to an available Excitation Filter, which is part of this Filter Cube.

children.name: Standard Dichroic Ref

children.name: Emission Filter Ref

children.name: Filter Set
children.description: This element represents a FilterSet that was used for the acquisition of this Channel. A Filter Set is typically composed of one Excitation Filter, one Emission Filter and a Dichroic. They might either be pre-assembled by the Manufacturer and purchased as a whole or they can be custom assembled on site depending on the needs of the user.

children.name: Transmission Wavelength Range Settings
children.description: This element records the range of Transmission Wavelengths that were set for this optical component during the Acquisition of this Image. It also records the maximum amount of light transmitted. Multiple of this elements can be used.

children.name: Cut On
children.description: CutOn is the Wavelength below which the response rate is below 50%. In case a CutOn Wavelength is not available, such as in the case of a ShortPass or Notch configuration, please enter 000.

children.name: Cut Off
children.description: CutOff is the Wavelength below which the response rate is below 50%. In case a CutOff Wavelength is not available, such as in the case of a LongPass configuration, please enter 000.

children.name: Transmittance
children.description: This field records the maximum amount of light this optical component is capable of transmitting in this Wavelength Range, at the peak Wavelength. It is represented as a fractional value from 0.0 to 1.0.

children.name: Reflection Wavelength Range Settings
children.description: This element records the range of Reflection Wavelengths that were set for this optical component during the Acquisition of this Image. It also records the maximum amount of light transmitted. Multiple of this elements can be used.

children.name: Cut On
children.description: CutOn is the Wavelength below which the response rate is below 50%. In case a CutOn Wavelength is not available, such as in the case of a ShortPass or Notch configuration, please enter 000.

children.name: Cut Off
children.description: CutOff is the Wavelength below which the response rate is below 50%. In case a CutOff Wavelength is not available, such as in the case of a LongPass configuration, please enter 000.

children.name: Reflectance
children.description: This field records the maximum amount of light this optical component is capable of reflecting in this Wavelength Range, at the peak Wavelength. It is represented as a fractional value from 0.0 to 1.0.

children.name: Diffraction Grating Settings
children.description: This element holds the Setting applied to a Diffraction Grating used during the Acquisition of this Channel as well as a reference (the ID attribute) to the Diffraction Grating in question.

children.name: ID
children.description: This is the Unique Identifier of the device used in this case.

children.name: Diffraction Efficiency
children.description: This is a measure of the performance efficiency of this device. It's a measure of how much optical power is diffracted into a designated direction compared to the power incident onto the diffractive element.

children.name: Transmission Wavelength Range Settings
children.description: This element records the range of Transmission Wavelengths that were set for this optical component during the Acquisition of this Image. It also records the maximum amount of light transmitted. Multiple of this elements can be used.

children.name: Cut On
children.description: CutOn is the Wavelength below which the response rate is below 50%. In case a CutOn Wavelength is not available, such as in the case of a ShortPass or Notch configuration, please enter 000.

children.name: Cut Off
children.description: CutOff is the Wavelength below which the response rate is below 50%. In case a CutOff Wavelength is not available, such as in the case of a LongPass configuration, please enter 000.

children.name: Transmittance
children.description: This field records the maximum amount of light this optical component is capable of transmitting in this Wavelength Range, at the peak Wavelength. It is represented as a fractional value from 0.0 to 1.0.

children.name: Reflection Wavelength Range Settings
children.description: This element records the range of Reflection Wavelengths that were set for this optical component during the Acquisition of this Image. It also records the maximum amount of light transmitted. Multiple of this elements can be used.

children.name: Reflectance

children.name: Pinhole Settings
children.description: This element holds the Setting applied to a Pinhole used during the Acquisition of this Channel as well as a reference (the ID attribute) to the Pinhole in question.

children.name: ID

children.name: Aperture
children.description: This refers to the Diameter of the Pin Hole's Aperture that was set for this Acquisition, expressed in Airy units.

children.name: Shutter Settings
children.description: This element holds the Setting applied to a Shutter used in the Light Path during the acquisition of this Channel as well as a reference (the ID attribute) to the Shutter in question.

children.name: ID
children.description: This is the Unique Identifier of the device used in this case.

children.name: Open During ZMovement
children.description: This field captures whether this Shutter was open (True) or closed (False) during Z-movement between successive Z-planes in this Image.

children.name: Aperture Diameter
children.description: This field records the actual physical Aperture Diameter that was used for this Shutter during the Acquisition of this Channel.

children.name: Mode
children.description: This field records the operation mode that was applied to this Shutter during the Acquisition of this Channel. The Fast mode provides the quickest open and close action sequence for which the shutter is capable. The Soft intensity without affecting wavelength is accommodated by a Neutral Density shutter mode (for more details see https://www.microscopyu.com/applications/live-cell-imaging/the-automatic-microscope).

children.name: Detector Settings Group
children.description: This element holds the Setting applied to the Detector used for the Acquisition of this Channel as well as a reference to the Detector (the ID attribute) in question.

children.name: Generic Detector Settings
children.description: This element refers to the Generic Detector that was used to Acquire this Channel and it holds the Settings that were applied to the Detector during this Acquisition.

children.name: ID
children.description: A Unique Identifier for this component.

children.name: Effective Bit Depth
children.description: If selectable, this field reports the Effective Bit Depth that was used by the Detector's Analog to Digital Converter (ADC; also referred to as Digitizer) for the Acquisition of this Channel.

children.name: Analog Gain
children.description: This field reports the Analog Gain (also known as Inherent Gain or simply as Gain) that was applied during the Acquisition of this Image. The Analog Gain is a property Inherent to the Detector and it specifies how many electrons correspond to 1 signal unit (photon) detected. Gain is reported in terms of electrons/ADU (analog-to-digital unit). A gain of 8 means that the Detector digitizes the light signal so that each ADU corresponds to 8 photoelectrons. NOTE: In the case of Point Detectors the value of Analog Gain can be set freely by the user. Conversely, in the case of Cameras, it is common for the Gain to be preset by the Manufacturer to minimize noise. In this case, either the Analog Gain cannot be changed or the user is given the choice of selecting between a few preset Analog Gains values upon Acquisition. Finally, several Cameras might have so-called Automatic Gain Control (AGC or AutoGain) that will automatically adjusts so the full range of the Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) is used. Unless you can turn off the AGC, the use of these Cameras are highly discouraged for quantitative work.

children.name: Amplification Gain
children.description: This field reports the Amplification Gain (i.e., electronic gain as apposed to the inherent Gain) that was set during the Acquisition of this Image. Amplification Gain in general measures of the ability of a Detector to add energy to the input light signal therefore increasing its power or amplitude. Higher Amplification Gain levels amplify the signal and result in greater brightness, contrast and background signal. Examples include: 1) the Electron Multiplication (EM) gain (as opposed to the inherent Analog Gain) for an EMCCD camera. 2) The Gain introduced by the Micro-Channel Plate in an Intensified CCD or CMOS camera. 3) The amplification of the electron current provided by a Photomultiplier Tube, and measured as the ratio of the input photoelectron current emitted by the photocatode and the current reaching the anode. 4) The signal amplification provided by an Avalache Photo Diode (APD).

children.name: Offset
children.description: If available, this field reports the actual Offset that was set for this Detector during the Acquisition of this Channel. Offset is an electronic baseline added to the output signal generated by the sensor to ensure that the resulting signal level always correspond to a positive number of counts. Offset defines the black level setting of the image and it should be adjusted so that the lowest signals are near the detection threshold. NOTE: Please note that most modern Cameras do not have the possibility of setting the Offset.

children.name: Offset Mode
children.description: This field describes the way in which the Voltage Offset of the Detector is reported. It typically is expressed as an Arbitrary-units number, a Scaled percent (-100 to 100), or a a Voltage.

children.name: Map Annotation
children.description: A Map Annotation. The contents of this is a list of Key/Value pairs.

children.name: ID

children.name: Point Detector Settings
children.description: This element refers to the Point Detector used to acquire this Channel and it holds the Settings that were applied to the Point Detector during this Acquisition.

children.name: ID
children.description: A Unique Identifier for this component.

children.name: Effective Bit Depth
children.description: If selectable, this field reports the Effective Bit Depth that was used by the Detector's Analog to Digital Converter (ADC; also referred to as Digitizer) for the Acquisition of this Channel.

children.name: Analog Gain
children.description: This field reports the Analog Gain (also known as Inherent Gain or simply as Gain) that was applied during the Acquisition of this Image. The Analog Gain is a property Inherent to the Detector and it specifies how many electrons correspond to 1 signal unit (photon) detected. Gain is reported in terms of electrons/ADU (analog-to-digital unit). A gain of 8 means that the Detector digitizes the light signal so that each ADU corresponds to 8 photoelectrons. NOTE: In the case of Point Detectors the value of Analog Gain can be set freely by the user. Conversely, in the case of Cameras, it is common for the Gain to be preset by the Manufacturer to minimize noise. In this case, either the Analog Gain cannot be changed or the user is given the choice of selecting between a few preset Analog Gains values upon Acquisition. Finally, several Cameras might have so-called Automatic Gain Control (AGC or AutoGain) that will automatically adjusts so the full range of the Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) is used. Unless you can turn off the AGC, the use of these Cameras are highly discouraged for quantitative work.

children.name: Amplification Gain
children.description: This field reports the Amplification Gain (i.e., electronic gain as apposed to the inherent Gain) that was set during the Acquisition of this Image. Amplification Gain in general measures of the ability of a Detector to add energy to the input light signal therefore increasing its power or amplitude. Higher Amplification Gain levels amplify the signal and result in greater brightness, contrast and background signal. Examples include: 1) the Electron Multiplication (EM) gain (as opposed to the inherent Analog Gain) for an EMCCD camera. 2) The Gain introduced by the Micro-Channel Plate in an Intensified CCD or CMOS camera. 3) The amplification of the electron current provided by a Photomultiplier Tube, and measured as the ratio of the input photoelectron current emitted by the photocatode and the current reaching the anode. 4) The signal amplification provided by an Avalache Photo Diode (APD).

children.name: Offset
children.description: If available, this field reports the actual Offset that was set for this Detector during the Acquisition of this Channel. Offset is an electronic baseline added to the output signal generated by the sensor to ensure that the resulting signal level always correspond to a positive number of counts. Offset defines the black level setting of the image and it should be adjusted so that the lowest signals are near the detection threshold. NOTE: Please note that most modern Cameras do not have the possibility of setting the Offset.

children.name: Offset Mode
children.description: This field describes the way in which the Voltage Offset of the Detector is reported. It typically is expressed as an Arbitrary-units number, a Scaled percent (-100 to 100), or a a Voltage.

children.name: Voltage
children.description: This field refers to the Voltage settings applied to the Photomultiplier Tube (PMT) for the Acquisition of this Channel (e.g., voltage = 244). The higher the supplied Voltage, the greater the amplification of the current generated by the PMT. While this increases the sensitivity, it also increases the background in the resulting image.

children.name: Photon Counting
children.description: This boolean indicates whether this Photomultiplier Tube was operated in photon-counting mode (True) or not (False), during the Acquisition of this Channel.

Examples include: 1) the Electron Multiplication (EM) gain (as opposed to the inherent Analog Gain) for an EMCCD camera. 2) The Gain introduced by the Micro-Channel Plate in an Intensified CCD or CMOS camera. 3) The amplification of the electron current provided by a Photomultiplier Tube, and measured as the ratio of the input photoelectron current emitted by the photocatode and the current reaching the anode. 4) The signal amplification provided by an Avalache Photo Diode (APD).

children.name: Offset
children.description: If available, this field reports the actual Offset that was set for this Detector during the Acquisition of this Channel. Offset is an electronic baseline added to the output signal generated by the sensor to ensure that the resulting signal level always correspond to a positive number of counts. Offset defines the black level setting of the image and it should be adjusted so that the lowest signals are near the detection threshold. NOTE: Please note that most modern Cameras do not have the possibility of setting the Offset.

children.name: Offset Mode
children.description: This field describes the way in which the Voltage Offset of the Detector is reported. It typically is expressed as an Arbitrary-units number, a Scaled percent (-100 to 100), or a a Voltage.

children.name: Binning
children.description: This field represents the number of adjacent pixels whose electric charge is combined in the resulting Image (e.g. binning = 2x2). Generally, binning increases read-out rates, signal-to-noise ratio, and relative sensitivity, while it reduces the spatial resolution.

children.name: Pixel Read Out Rate
children.description: This represents the effective Pixel Readout Rate used for the Acquisition of this Channel. This value is defined as the inverse of the time required to digitize a single pixel (also referred to as serial conversion time). Pixel readout rate can be considered similar to a baud rate as it depends on the bytes per second that can be read from the detector and written on disk. Readout rates are usually given in pixels/second (e.g., 500 kilopixels/second).

children.name: Sensor Exposure Mode
children.description: In case of a CMOS Camera and if selectable, this field reports the sensor Exposure modality used for the Acquisition of this Channel. In the Rolling Exposure mode adjacent rows of the array are exposed at slightly different times as the readout 'waves' sweep through each half of the sensor. In the Global Exposure mode (i.e., 'Snapshot' exposure mode) all pixels of the array are exposed simultaneously, thus enabling 'freeze frame' capture of fast moving or fast changing events. In this modality the CMOS Camera behaves like an interline CCD sensor.

children.name: Integration
children.description: This field represents the number of sequential frames that have been averaged to obtain this image, to improve the overall signal-to-noise ratio.

children.name: Frame Rate
children.description: Frame Rate is the inverse of the time needed for the CCD to acquire a single Frame (or Plane) and then completely read that image out. Frame Rate is typically expressed in frames per second (fps). Often, the Frame Rate can be approximately calculated from the total number of pixels and the readout rate, combined with the total exposure time. This field represents the effective Frame Rate used for the Acquisition of this Image.

children.name: Operating Temperature
children.description: This field represents the effective temperature the sensor was kept at during the Acquisition of this image.

children.description: A Map Annotation. The contents of this is a list of Key/Value pairs.
